work_history___job_1.duties_job_1:
Served as a Senior Customer Support Specialist in the member services department for a regional healthcare network. Handled an average of 40–60 inbound calls and 15–20 email inquiries per day from patients, providers, and insurance representatives regarding benefits, billing questions, provider networks, and appointment scheduling. Specialized in resolving complex claims and billing discrepancies by reviewing explanation of benefits (EOBs), cross-checking CPT and ICD-10 codes in the internal system, and coordinating with the claims processing team to issue adjustments, refunds, or corrected claims when appropriate.

Acted as an informal team lead for a rotating group of 5–7 junior representatives: helped monitor call queues, provided side-by-side coaching, and created quick-reference guides and FAQ documents based on recurring member issues. Regularly trained new hires on our CRM (Salesforce Health Cloud), electronic health record portal, and phone system, walking them through real-world examples of tricky calls (e.g., appeals, prior authorization questions, or coverage denials) so they could hear how I framed explanations in plain language.

Managed high-stress escalations, including calls from frustrated or anxious patients dealing with surprise bills, coverage denials, or time-sensitive procedures. Focused on de-escalation techniques such as active listening, repeating back concerns, and clearly outlining next steps. For example, I often walked members through filing formal appeals, drafting appeal letters with them over the phone when they struggled with wording, and explaining realistic timelines and what documentation they needed from their providers.

Maintained detailed, accurate documentation for every interaction, including call summaries, actions taken, and follow-up tasks, to ensure continuity of service. Frequently coordinated three-way calls between members, providers, and our internal departments to resolve issues in a single interaction when possible. Also monitored my own performance metrics (average handle time, first-call resolution rate, quality scores, and member satisfaction surveys) and consistently ranked in the top 10–15% of the department.

In addition to day-to-day support, participated in quarterly process-improvement meetings where front-line staff could share recurring pain points and suggest changes. I advocated for revising some confusing language in member letters and updating outdated scripts that did not reflect how our online portal actually worked. A few of my suggestions were adopted, including adding clearer step-by-step instructions for downloading EOBs online and simplifying the language used to describe deductibles and out-of-pocket maximums.
work_history___job_1.reason_for_leaving_job_1:
I am seeking a role with more opportunities for advancement and broader responsibilities, ideally in a setting where I can move into a formal team lead or training position. After nearly five years in the same department, I reached a point where my growth was limited—internal promotions into supervisor roles have been very slow, and my last two applications were passed over due to seniority-based decisions rather than performance. I am proud of the work I have done, especially mentoring new team members and helping improve our internal documentation, but I feel ready to take on more responsibility and have a clearer career path.

Additionally, Northwest Health Systems recently announced restructuring and possible consolidation of some call center functions to another region, which created uncertainty about long-term stability in my current role. Although my position was not immediately impacted, the environment has become more tense, with hiring freezes and reduced overtime. Rather than waiting to see how those changes play out, I decided it was a better time to proactively look for a position where I can continue to grow, deepen my skills, and ideally contribute to building better processes and training from the outset.

work_history___job_2.duties_job_2:
Worked as a front-line Customer Service Representative for a mid-sized outdoor gear retailer that sold camping, hiking, and climbing equipment both online and in-store. My role was a hybrid of in-store floor support and contact center duties, depending on the season and staffing needs. On the retail side, I greeted customers, asked open-ended questions to understand their needs (such as what type of trip they were planning or what experience level they had), and recommended appropriate gear. I often helped parents and beginners navigate confusing product categories like sleeping bag temperature ratings, backpack sizing, or waterproof vs. water-resistant outerwear, using simple comparisons and personal examples from my own hiking experiences.

On the contact center side, I handled phone, email, and live chat inquiries related to online orders, returns, backorders, and product information. I processed returns and exchanges within the point-of-sale and order management systems, issued prepaid return labels, and carefully explained our return policy so customers knew what to expect regarding refunds and timelines. I also helped track lost or delayed shipments by coordinating with UPS and USPS, and followed up with customers once replacement orders were sent out.

Because I developed a reputation for being patient and detail-oriented, I was frequently asked to assist with more complex situations, such as warranty claims for defective gear, high-value orders requiring extra verification, and situations where customers felt misled by online descriptions. I would gather photos, receipts, and detailed information from customers, then work with our purchasing team and vendors to determine whether repairs, replacements, or store credit were appropriate. I kept customers updated throughout the process so they did not feel forgotten.

In slower periods, I helped with inventory counts, restocking, and merchandising—especially in the camping and backpacking sections where I was most knowledgeable. I created small handwritten tip cards for popular items (e.g., how to break in new hiking boots, how to store a down sleeping bag) that customers often commented on and took photos of. I also informally trained new hires on our POS system and on how to have consultative conversations rather than just asking, "Can I help you find something?"
work_history___job_2.reason_for_leaving_job_2:
I left Cascade Outdoor Retail Co. to transition into a more stable, full-time office environment with regular weekday hours and benefits, which my previous retail position could not consistently provide. While I enjoyed the customer interaction and the product knowledge aspect of the job, my schedule fluctuated a lot from week to week, including late evenings, weekends, and holiday shifts. This made it difficult to plan family commitments and pursue additional training or coursework.

In late 2019, I began looking for roles that would allow me to use my customer service skills in a setting where there was clearer potential for long-term growth and more predictable hours. When I received an offer from Northwest Health Systems for a full-time position with better health benefits, paid time off, and a set Monday–Friday schedule, I decided to make the switch. I left on good terms, gave a full three weeks’ notice to help cover the busy winter season, and even assisted with training two new associates before my last day.

work_history___job_3.duties_job_3:
Provided part-time administrative support for a small, two-dentist family dental practice that served a mix of children, adults, and seniors. Split responsibilities between front-desk reception, scheduling, and basic office administration. Greeted patients as they arrived, checked them in, verified insurance information, and ensured all health history and consent forms were completed and updated. Answered incoming calls, scheduled and rescheduled appointments, and maintained a daily schedule for both dentists and the hygienists using Dentrix practice management software.

Handled insurance verification and pre-authorization for common procedures such as crowns, root canals, and periodontal treatments. This involved logging into various insurance portals, confirming eligibility, checking annual maximums and remaining benefits, and documenting coverage details in the patient record so the clinical team could discuss treatment options with accurate estimates. I also prepared and submitted insurance claims, attached digital X-rays and intraoral photos when required, and tracked unpaid claims to follow up on delays or denials.

Managed basic billing tasks, including printing and mailing monthly statements, taking payments over the phone, and explaining balances to patients who were confused about what their insurance covered. When patients were overwhelmed by unexpected balances, I helped set up simple payment plans and made sure they understood our policies. I learned to navigate sensitive conversations in a calm, respectful way, especially with older patients who were not comfortable using online systems.

On the office administration side, I maintained paper and digital filing systems for treatment plans, referrals, and correspondence with specialists. I ordered office and dental supplies, keeping a simple spreadsheet to track typical usage so we did not run out of essentials. During slower periods, I helped clean and tidy the waiting area, updated children’s books and magazines, and put together small take-home goodie bags for kids after their appointments.

Because it was a small practice, I often stepped into whatever role was needed in the moment, whether that meant running to the back to let the assistants know a patient had arrived early, helping a nervous child pick out a toothbrush color, or staying a little late to finish entering notes from a busy afternoon.
work_history___job_3.reason_for_leaving_job_3:
I left Sunnyside Family Dental Clinic when my hours could no longer be increased beyond part-time and I needed a full-time role to support myself. I originally took the position while I was finishing community college coursework and it fit well with my class schedule, but after graduating and moving closer to Portland, the limited hours and commute made it less sustainable.

I spoke openly with Dr. Chang about my need for a full-time opportunity, and although she was very supportive, the practice simply did not have the patient volume or budget to add more administrative hours at that time. After a few months of trying to piece together extra income with temporary shifts at a nearby coffee shop, I decided it was better to look for a single, full-time position. When I received an offer from Cascade Outdoor Retail Co. with consistent 35–40 hour weeks, I accepted and provided several weeks’ notice so the clinic could hire and train another part-time assistant.

work_history___job_4.duties_job_4:
Started as a barista at a busy independent coffee shop and bakery located near a commuter route, and was later promoted to a shift lead position after about a year. As a barista, prepared a full range of espresso drinks, brewed coffee, teas, and specialty seasonal beverages while maintaining consistency in taste and presentation. Took customer orders, handled cash and card transactions, and kept the front counter and seating area clean and welcoming. Memorized many regular customers’ usual orders, which helped build rapport and a sense of community.

As a shift lead, I was responsible for opening or closing the shop, depending on the schedule. Opening duties included arriving around 5:00 AM, calibrating the espresso machine, setting up pastry displays, brewing the first batches of coffee, and ensuring that the cash drawer was counted and balanced. Closing duties involved reconciling the register, preparing bank deposits, cleaning equipment, restocking supplies, and making sure food safety and cleanliness standards were met (wiping down surfaces, labeling and dating items, and discarding anything past its shelf life).

I helped train new baristas on our drink recipes, food handling procedures, and customer service standards. This included walking them through the menu, demonstrating how to handle rush periods by prioritizing orders and communicating clearly with coworkers, and coaching them on how to handle difficult interactions (for example, when an order was made incorrectly or when a customer was in a hurry and visibly frustrated). I emphasized the importance of staying calm, offering solutions, and not taking raised voices personally.

In addition to front-of-house responsibilities, I assisted the owner with simple inventory tracking for coffee beans, milk, syrups, and bakery items, noting patterns such as which pastries sold out early and which tended to sit. I also suggested adding a small whiteboard by the register for daily specials and a punch-card loyalty program, both of which were implemented and received positive feedback from regular customers.

This job was fast-paced and physically demanding, but it taught me how to manage multiple tasks at once, stay friendly even during early-morning rushes, and work smoothly with coworkers in tight spaces. It also gave me my first experience handling basic supervisory tasks, like assigning side work and giving gentle feedback when cleaning or drink quality did not meet expectations.
work_history___job_4.reason_for_leaving_job_4:
I left Riverside Coffee & Bakery in order to focus on my administrative career path and to take a position that aligned more closely with my long-term goals. While I appreciated the experience and the relationships I built there, the early-morning and weekend-heavy schedule was difficult to maintain once I began taking more intensive community college courses in the evenings. I also realized that, although I enjoyed customer service, I was more interested in developing office, organizational, and clerical skills that would open doors to future roles in healthcare or business settings.

When an opportunity arose to work part-time as an administrative assistant at Sunnyside Family Dental Clinic, I decided to make the transition. I gave the owner of Riverside almost a month of notice, helped train two new hires on opening and closing procedures, and created a simple checklist for weekend shift leads so the handoff would be smooth after I left.
